content:
Pull requests (PRs) are similarly enriched with context. An AI raising a PR (or a human) should begin the PR description with a summary of changes and a bullet list of references: e.g. “This PR implements the **Seed World** function as per [oai_citation:10‡autodesk.com](https://www.autodesk.com/solutions/media-entertainment/procedural-generation#:~:text=Procedural%20generation%20is%20a%20technique,content%2C%20including%20landscapes%20and%20characters) in the Procedural Generation spec, and addresses the requirements in ARCHIVE `VISION-META-SPEC-OS-0001-v1_0` section 4.2. Closes #issue (if using issue trackers).” By referencing archive IDs, we ensure the discussion around the PR can be informed by looking at the original design rationale. Once a PR is merged, we might even automate adding an entry to a changelog in $ARCHIVE_ROOT/meta or updating a status in the corresponding spec (like marking it as implemented).

Finally, the GitHub organization being neutral and long-term oriented means we avoid naming things after ephemeral team names or brands – it’s all generic (like using OS, tools, worlds, etc., rather than a codename that might lose meaning). $ORG_ID itself is a placeholder; we assume something simple like “CreativeOS” or similar. The idea is that this OS could be open-source or at least structured in a way that multiple people/agents can collaborate asynchronously, with Git providing the concurrency control and history.
context: From document: # Blueprint for a Self-Evolving Creative Operating System — chunk 30 (chunk 30/38)
